title: NOC 74204 – Utility maintenance workers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 74204 professionals are the essential multi-skilled technicians who maintain the integrity of Canada’s public and private utility networks. As a TEER 4 occupation, these workers perform a variety of maintenance and repair tasks on water and waste-water systems, gas mains, and electrical utility infrastructure. In 2026, as Canada aggressively upgrades its municipal infrastructure to be "Climate-Resilient," Utility Maintenance Workers are classified as Critical Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a key target for the Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot and the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level4Major Group74 – Mail and message distribution, transport equipment operators and maintenanceSub-Major Group742 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersMinor Group7420 – Transport equipment operators, utility maintenance and related workersNOC Code74204

duties: System Maintenance: Inspect, maintain, and repair water and waste-water systems, including water mains, valves, hydrants, and service connections.
Leak Detection: Use specialized acoustic and digital equipment to locate gas and water leaks in underground utility lines.
Installation: Install and replace water meters and gas meters in residential and commercial properties.
Equipment Operation: Operate specialized utility vehicles and equipment such as vacuum trucks, high-pressure flushers, and trenching machinery.
Emergency Response: Respond to urgent utility failures, such as water main breaks or gas odors, to isolate faults and perform immediate repairs.
Documentation: Maintain detailed digital logs of inspections, repairs performed, and materials used in company or municipal asset management systems.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is the standard requirement.
Training: Several months of on-the-job training are typically provided by the municipality or utility company.

Certifications:A valid Class 5 Driver’s License is mandatory; a Class 3 (heavy truck) is highly preferred.Water Quality/Distribution Certification (Level 1 or 2) is often required for waterworks roles.Confined Space Entry and First Aid certifications are standard safety requirements in 2026.
A valid Class 5 Driver’s License is mandatory; a Class 3 (heavy truck) is highly preferred.
Water Quality/Distribution Certification (Level 1 or 2) is often required for waterworks roles.
Confined Space Entry and First Aid certifications are standard safety requirements in 2026.
Physical strength for manual labor and the ability to work outdoors in all weather conditions are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, unionized municipal utility workers in major cities often earn annual salaries between $68,000 and $92,000 inclusive of overtime and emergency standby premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate that the shift toward "Net-Zero Water" and hydrogen-ready gas grids will require more technical support from maintenance crews. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), Retirements will create over 12,000 job openings across Canada through 2033, ensuring long-term demand for skilled utility talent.
labour_market_demand: The demand for utility maintenance workers in 2026 is Strong, particularly in high-growth urban corridors and aging municipal districts. In Ontario, British Columbia, and Alberta, municipal governments and private utility firms are facing a "Replacement Crisis" as senior technicians retire. According to 2026 Job Bank data, there is a persistent labor shortage for workers specialized in leak detection technology and trenchless pipe repair, especially in the Greater Toronto Area and the BC Lower Mainland.
The "Smart Utility" Transition: Gain experience working with advanced digital sensors, IoT-enabled flow meters, and robotic pipe inspection cameras.
Public Sector Benefits: Many roles are within municipal governments, offering superior job security, union protection, and comprehensive federal/provincial pension plans.
Diverse Skill Development: This role provides exposure to plumbing, electrical, and heavy equipment operation, creating a versatile technical profile.

Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 4 role is a direct beneficiary of the 2026 federal and provincial pilots designed to retain essential infrastructure workers who have at least one year of Canadian experience.
pathways: New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, targeting those in TEER 4 infrastructure roles with one year of experience.
Saskatchewan SINP (Hard-to-Fill Skills): A high-priority route for utility workers with a valid job offer in the province’s municipal sector.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Ideal for technicians currently employed in the province with a valid job offer from a utility provider.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for those with a job offer from a designated municipal employer in an Atlantic province.
